text: 401 Unauthorized — the request lacks valid authentication. Check that your token or API key is present, not expired, and sent in the correct header (usually Authorization: Bearer <token>).
403 Forbidden — the server understood the request but refuses to authorise it. Your credentials are valid but lack the required permissions. Check roles, scopes, or resource-level access controls.
404 Not Found — the resource does not exist at this URL. Check for typos in the path, verify the resource was created, and confirm you are hitting the correct environment (dev/staging/prod).

429 Too Many Requests — the rate limit has been exceeded. Back off and retry after the duration specified in the Retry-After header. Consider caching responses or batching requests to stay within limits.
500 Internal Server Error — the server encountered an unexpected condition. Check server logs for the stack trace. This is a bug on the server side, not the client.

CORS error — the browser blocked the request because the server did not include the required Access-Control-Allow-Origin header. Add CORS middleware on the server, or configure it to allow your origin explicitly.

Request timed out — the server did not respond within the allowed window. Check for slow database queries, downstream service latency, or network issues. Increase timeout thresholds only after diagnosing the root cause.
SSL/TLS error — the certificate is invalid, expired, or untrusted. Verify the certificate chain, check the expiry date, and ensure the hostname matches the certificate's Common Name or SAN.
